back_translation: If I am able to speak in the languages of human beings and of angels, but I do not have love, I become like a gong that makes loud sound, or like a cymbal that makes clanging sound.

And if I have the gift of prophesying, and understand all mysteries and all knowledge; and if I have full faith such as to be able to move mountains — but I do not have love — I am only something with no value at all.
And if I give all my possessions to feed the poor, and if I hand over my body in order to be burned — but I do not have love — I do not receive any benefit at all.
Love is patient-and-long-suffering. Love acts with a generous heart. Love does not envy. It does not boast. It does not become puffed-up.
It does not behave inappropriately. It does not seek its own benefit. It is not provoked-to-anger easily. It does not keep a record of others' wrongs.
It does not rejoice in unrighteousness, but it rejoices in truth.
It protects all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things.
Love never fails. But if it is the prophesying, it will come to its end. If it is the various languages, they will stop. If it is knowledge, it will be made to cease in its operation.
Because we know only in part, and we prophesy only in part.
But when that which is complete arrives, the thing that is in part will be made to cease in its operation.
When I myself was still a child, I spoke as a child, thought as a child, used reasoning as a child. When I became an adult, I set aside the things that belong to a child.

Because at present we see as a hazy image in the mirror; but in that time, we will see one another face-to-face truly. At present I know only in part; but in that time I will know fully, just as I myself also have received-being-known.
Now: faith, hope, and love — these three remain. But among these three, love is the greatest.
